Centres of Mass and Moments of Inertia

January 19, 2022 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Double Integrals Special case: Ω is a rectangle I How can we find the area of a bounded subset Ω in the plane? I First we let Ω be a rectangle R = [a, b] × [c, d] and let f : R → R be a bounded function. I If f is a continuous and positive function defined on Ω, I Partition the interval [a, b] into m subintervals [xi−1 , xi ] then the equation z = f (x, y) represents a surface that by choosing a = x0 < x1 < x2 < · · · < xm = b. lies above Ω. What is the volume of the solid that is bounded below by Ω and above by the surface I Partition the interval [c, d] into n subintervals [yj−1 , yj ] z = f (x, y)? by choosing c = y0 < y1 < y2 < · · · < yn = d. I To answer these questions, we study the integral of I These partitions induce a partition f : Ω → R, denoted by P = { Rij : 1 ≤ i ≤ m, 1 ≤ j ≤ n } of the rectangle R Z into many small rectangles f (x, y) dA, Ω Rij = [xi−1 , xi ] × [yj−1 , yj ].

where dA stands for “with respect to area”. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . The upper and lower sums Double integral as the limit of Riemann sums I Summing over i and j should lead to Let |P| denote the maximum length of the sides of the Z rectangles in P. Thus, L(f, P) ≤ f dA ≤ U (f, P) R ∆xi ≤ |P| and ∆yj ≤ |P| for i and j. where the lower (Riemann) sum corresponding to the partition P = {Rij } is Definition 1 The bounded function f : R → R is Riemann integrable if X m X n L(f, P) = fijmin ∆Aij , U (f, P) − L(f, P) → 0 as |P| → 0. i=1 j=1

In this case, the double integral of f is the unique real and the upper (Riemann) sum is number I such that X m X n U (f, P) = fijmax ∆Aij . L(f, P) ≤ I ≤ U (f, P) for every partition P of R. i=1 j=1 . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .   How to compute? When f is Riemann integrable, we denote its double integral over R by Z ZZ I As with one variable functions, using upper and lower I= f dA or I = f dA. sums to calculate integrals is tedious. R R R The idea is that dA is an infinitesimal area, and is the limit I Bad news: There is no Fundamental Theorem of Calculus of a sum. to replace the limiting process with a symbolic manipulation.

Strategy to evaluate double integrals Example 8 1. Draw the integration domain. Calculate Z πZ π sin y 2. Trace a line parallel to the axis of the inner variable to dy dx. 0 x y determine the inner limits. 3. Consider how much you can move the tracer line to determine the outer limits. 4. Check that the outer limits must be constants and the inner limits can be constants or functions of the outer variable. Note I Sometimes it is easier to integrate in one order than the other. I Make sure to find the correct inner and outer limits when reversing the order. A sketch of Ω is useful and sometimes essential. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . Jacobian determinant (optional) I The result that dA = dx dy = r dr dθ (page 26) is a special case of a general rule for substitutions in double integrals. Mass of a lamina Centre of mass I In this course we will only examine the mass and centre I The centre of mass of an object is the point where the of mass of laminas, that is, flat sheets of material with mass is evenly balanced in all directions. negligible thickness. I If the mass is uniformly distributed (constant density) I If the (areal) density (mass per unit area) of the lamina is then the centre of mass will be the same as the centroid, given by δ(x, y) then the mass in an infinitesimal area dA the geometric centre of the object. equals I In general, however, the centre of mass differs from the dm = δ(x, y) dA (mass = density × area). centroid. Knowing the centre of mass greatly simplifies calculations of the dynamics of the object because we can I The total mass of the object with a 2D shape given by Ω often pretend that all of the mass is concentrated at that is the integral of the density, point. XX ZZ I Think of a thin tile or plate. The centre of mass (x̄, ȳ) of M = lim lim δ(xi , yj ) ∆Aij = δ(x, y) dA. such an object is the unique point where you could ∆x→0 ∆y→0 i j Ω balance the plate on the tip of your finger. . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .  Moment of mass I For a lamina Ω with areal density δ(x, y), we calculate the I Imagine that we place an object of mass m on a lever a first moment about the y-axis by integrating over the first distance x from the y-axis (x = 0). Then the mass would moments of the infinitesimal masses dm = δ(x, y) dA, tend to rotate the lever around the y-axis. ZZ ZZ I This tendency is measured by the first moment of mass My = x dm = x δ(x, y) dA. about the y-axis, Ω Ω

My = xm (first moment = distance × mass). I The corresponding first moment about the x-axis is ZZ ZZ I The heavier the mass, the greater the first moment. But Mx = y dm = y δ(x, y) dA. we can also increase the first moment by moving further Ω Ω

out along the x-axis. A long lever has a greater moment than a short lever.

Example 24 Find √ the mass and the centre of mass of a lamina bounded by y = 1 − x2 and the x axis assuming: 1. uniform density δ(x, y) = 1; 2. variable density given by δ(x, y) = y; 3. variable density given by δ(x, y) = r.

Moment of inertia I The resistance of an object to acceleration is called inertia. The resistance of an object to angular acceleration is called the moment of inertia. I For a point mass m located a distance x from the origin, the moment of inertia for rotations about the y-axis is

moment of inertia = distance2 × mass = x2 m.

I Heavier objects have a larger moment of inertia — they are more resistant to rotations. But we can also increase the moment of inertia by moving the mass further from 4 the axis of rotation. This is how ice-skaters can control (1) M = π/2, Mx = 2/3, My = 0, (x̄, ȳ) = (0, 3π ) their spin by extending their arm or bringing them closer (2) M = 2/3, Mx = π/8, My = 0, (x̄, ȳ) = (0, 3π/16) 3 to their bodies. (3) M = π/3, Mx = 1/2, My = 0, (x̄, ȳ) = (0, 2π ) . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . . .

Example 25 I For a lamina Ω with density δ(x, y), the moments of Find the mass, centre of mass and the moment of inertia about inertia of the lamina about the x and y axes are the y-axis of a thin plate bounded by the curves y = x2 and ZZ ZZ y = 2x − x2 , with variable density given by δ(x, y) = x + 1. Ix = y δ(x, y) dA and Iy = x2 δ(x, y) dA. Ω Ω

These tell us how hard it is to rotate the lamina Ω about the x and y axes.

I The polar moment of inertia, ZZ Iz = Ix + Iy = (x2 + y 2 ) δ(x, y) dA, Ω | {z } r2
